content:
17 min read
Just now

--

We let the system from Part 1 run for weeks on a real 33-repo codebase, asked the agent to attack its own memory, closed the two open items, then measured everything.
New here? Start with Part 1: How We Gave Claude Code a Memory That Maintains Itself. This part stands on its own, but the ideas it builds on are there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

2022 — https://maps.app.goo.gl/BsN44YLALvrnDjR76Index

• 0. TLDR

• 1. Intro

• 2. The map lied anyway (just not where we expected)

• 3. The safety net, hardened

• 4. Superseded findings stop cluttering the map

• 5. Auto-promotion: memories that earn it

• 6. The index learns what things cost

• 7. Measuring it: the token study

• 8. The last liar: a summary that outlives its body

• The whole mechanism, in four pictures

• 9. Final notes

• 10. Install it in one command

0. TLDR
Part 1 gave an AI coding agent durable project memory: three folders, one-line summaries inside every file, and an index that is generated from the files so it can’t drift out of date.
Part 2 is what happened when that system met reality. We found holes, including files the index couldn’t see and a bootstrap prompt that itself went stale. We hardened the safety net, taught the index to hide superseded findings and to show the token price of everything it lists, and closed the two open items from Part 1: a usage log that lets memories earn their promotion with evidence instead of judgment, and staleness detection that fingerprints every doc so a body can’t silently drift away from its one-line summary.
Then we measured it on the real deployment: a ~4.6M-token codebase, ~6,500 tokens loaded per session, break-even on the first reused fact. And this time you don’t have to copy files out of an article. One curl | bash at the end installs the whole system into any project.

1. Intro
Part 1 ended with two confessions. The scripts could keep the index in sync with the summary lines, but nothing could tell whether a summary was still accurate after its doc changed. And promotion, meaning the move of a finding from MEMORY up into KNOWLEDGE, was pure judgment, with nothing recording whether a memory was being used at all.
This article closes both, and it starts with a confession of its own.
After weeks of real use on the SDK codebase from Part 1, we did something slightly recursive: we opened a session and asked the agent to review its own memory system. Ruthlessly. Find what’s broken, what’s brittle, what only looks solid. It found more than we were hoping for.

2. The map lied anyway (just not where we expected)
The whole point of Part 1 was an index that can’t lie, and the index didn’t. Two other things did.
The first was the bootstrap prompt. MAIN_PROMPT.md, the one file pasted at the start of every session, opened with a hand-written Root: line pointing at the folder. The project moved on disk. The line didn't. The system that makes indexes unable to lie was being launched by a prompt that lied.
The obvious fix is to correct the path. We did, and it was the wrong fix, because the interesting question isn’t why the path went stale. It’s why a hand-maintained path was there at all. Every other value in the system is derived: indexes are generated, scripts locate themselves. That one line was the last thing a human had to keep true, and it sat at the entry point, where being wrong is fatal.
It got worse when we looked at how the installer kept it true. A sed rewrote the line at install time. The line had since been reworded, and sed exits 0 when its pattern matches nothing. So the installer reported success, patched nothing, and shipped strangers a prompt pointing at a private path on my laptop. A silent no-op was guarding the one value nothing else could catch.
So the fix isn’t a better path, or a better sed. It's to have no path. The prompt now discovers its own root: walk up from the working directory, and at each ancestor look for a child folder that contains the framework, meaning a scripts/rebuild-index.sh and a KNOWLEDGE/. Match on contents, never on name.
Two things fell out of that, one expected and one not. The expected one is that there’s nothing left to patch at install time, so the class of bug is gone rather than fixed. The unexpected one is that since discovery never looks at the folder’s name, the name became free. Call it .agentic_context, brain, or notes, and rename it later if you like. A constraint we'd never questioned turned out to be an artifact of the bug, and it disappeared along with it.
(One caution, if you build the same thing: scan up, never down. Searching each ancestor’s whole subtree for the marker took 14.8 seconds from $HOME. Listing only each ancestor's immediate children and stat-ing the marker takes 0.029s. Five hundred times faster, and exactly as correct.)
The old rule survives as the fallback. If discovery finds nothing, the agent stops and says so plainly, rather than guessing, searching elsewhere, or helpfully recreating the folder. But it now fires when the folder is genuinely absent, instead of firing because someone moved it.
The second liar was the index itself, by omission. The rebuild script only looked one group-folder deep. Drop a doc at the folder root, or nest it one level too far, and it exists on disk but never appears in the index, silently. A skill folder missing its SKILL.md? Also invisible. No error anywhere.
Is there a better way?

3. The safety net, hardened
validate.sh grew from two checks to five. It now flags:

• Missing or empty summaries, as before.

• Multi-line summaries. The summary must be exactly one line followed by a blank line, or the index silently truncates it. (Embarrassingly, the example in Part 1 showed a summary wrapped across two lines. Don’t do that.)

• Malformed Used in: lines. Empty domains or duplicate entries would corrupt the auto-promotion count, so the validator fails them before rebuild.

• Strays. Any doc sitting where the index script can’t see it, whether at the folder root or in a skill folder without its SKILL.md, is now an error instead of a silent gap. (The "nested too deep" hole got the opposite fix: the rebuild script learned to recurse, so depth is supported rather than forbidden, and deeper docs get indexed under their subpath.)

• Stale indexes. If any content file is newer than its INDEX.md, validation fails and lists the changed files with an instruction: re-check that each file's summary still fits its body, then rebuild.
That last check quietly does double duty. The mechanical half of Part 1’s staleness item, index older than content, is detected automatically. The judgment half, meaning whether the summary still matches the body, at least gets pointed at. The system now tells you exactly which files to re-check and when, instead of hoping someone remembers. It also catches a lazier failure: an agent that skipped the rebuild step entirely. Drift used to be silent, and now it’s a red line in the output. At the time we thought this was the closest anyone could get to semantic staleness without a database. We were wrong, as chapter 8 explains, but it took living with the check to see why. It fires at the right moment, then forgets the question the instant you rebuild.
A postscript, on a lesson the safety net taught us later. The validator originally answered two different questions with one exit code: are the sources well-formed, and is the index in sync? The first is a precondition of rebuilding, the second a postcondition of it. The first strict consumer of the pipeline was a save flow that validates before rebuilding, exactly as it should, and it was guaranteed a false failure, because the file it just wrote is always newer than the index by definition. So the script grew a --content flag that runs only the gate, while the full check with freshness stays the session-start default. The general rule: source checks go before derivation, derived-state checks after. You usually only discover you've mixed them when a second caller reveals that the first one was compatible by coincidence.

4. Superseded findings stop cluttering the map
MEMORY has a rule from Part 1: never delete a finding, supersede it. Write the new one, mark the old one stale. Good for provenance, bad for the index. After a year, the list at the front of every consultation would be half tombstones.
Now a superseded finding gets a [STALE] prefix on its summary, and the rebuild script moves it into a superseded tail section at the bottom of the index: out of the active list, still on disk, still auditable. The index stays lean while the history stays complete.
One more rule fell out of real use. Before promoting a finding into KNOWLEDGE, check whether a KNOWLEDGE doc already covers it. If so, don’t promote. Fold anything new into that doc and mark the finding [STALE]. One source of truth. (We caught our own system about to violate this, with a [PROMOTE?] finding about repo paths that a [CORE] doc already answered.)

5. Auto-promotion: memories that earn it
Part 1 said promotion is decided by generality rather than frequency, and that a human confirms it. That’s still true. What changed is how candidates get found.
The insight is that generality sounds unmeasurable, but it isn’t. A finding consulted three times from the same module is frequent but narrow. A finding consulted from three different domains, say a build task, a testing task, and an architecture question, has proven its generality empirically. That’s the rule of three from refactoring, applied to knowledge.
So the system got a lightweight usage log, in the most low-tech way possible:

• Each finding may carry a Used in: line. When a finding answers a task, the agent appends that task's domain to it. One line of plain markdown, inside the file, so it stays the source of truth and travels with the folder.

• At rebuild time, the script counts distinct domains. At three or more, the finding’s index entry is automatically tagged as an auto-promotion candidate, with the domain count attached.
Notice where that flag lives: in the derived index, never in the source file. This is Part 1’s core principle applied to promotion itself. The flag is a computed fact, regenerated from evidence on every rebuild. Nobody has to remember to nominate a finding, because the evidence nominates it.
Here is what that looks like from the outside. This is a real session bootstrap. The agent self-heals the indexes, loads the core, and then, without being asked, surfaces the one finding that has earned its way onto the ballot.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The candidate is a repo map, project-paths/…all-local-git-repos.md, described as broadly useful. Nobody flagged it by hand. It accumulated Used in: domains by being genuinely useful, the rebuild counted them, and the index nominated it. What the agent does with the nomination is the part that matters: it asks. "Want it graduated into KNOWLEDGE? That's your decision, no action unless you approve."
Two guardrails keep this sane. The human still confirms the move, because folding a finding into KNOWLEDGE often means merging it with an existing doc, which needs judgment. And promotion does not imply [CORE]. A promoted doc is still lazy-loaded unless a human deliberately adds the always-load tag. Promotion into lazy KNOWLEDGE can be liberal and evidence-driven, while the core stays small and human-guarded.

6. The index learns what things cost
Once you start thinking in tokens (next chapter), one more derived fact begs to be in the index: the price of opening each document.
So the rebuild script now appends an estimated load cost to every entry, file size divided by four, the standard characters-per-token approximation:
- `project/Acronyms.md` — [CORE] Glossary of 80+ ecosystem acronyms… (~2.4k tok)
- `docs/DocsPortal.md` — Public documentation portals + build steps. (~38 tok)Same principle as everything else. It’s computed at rebuild time, from the f
